steps.title: A cleaner way to mix text and values

narration.default: When you mixed text and numbers earlier you used the plus sign to glue them together — and wrapped numbers in str so the plus would behave. It works but gets long fast. Today's tutorial: a cleaner way that's standard Python — the f-string. Real programmers reach for it almost every day.

Type three variables:

name = "Aman"
score = 85
total = 100

A name as text, a score and a total as numbers. The greeting we print will use all three.

Type the long way first, the way you've done before:

print("Hi, " + name + "! You scored " + str(score) + " out of " + str(total))

Notice the str around every number — without it, plus would refuse to mix text with numbers. Lots of plus signs, lots of quotes, easy to miss one. It works — it just isn't pretty.

Type the same greeting the new way:

print(f"Hi, {name}! You scored {score} out of {total}")

The little f right before the opening quote is the magic. Inside the quotes, anywhere you want a variable's value, write its name in curly braces. Python fills in each one for you. Numbers don't need str — Python sorts that out automatically. One sentence, no plus signs, no str.
Click Run. The terminal shows two identical greetings — same words, same numbers — produced two completely different ways. Same output, very different code.

Real Python programmers use f-strings everywhere — error messages, filenames, log lines, terminal output, web pages. The plus-glue still works, but f-strings read like English: a sentence with the variables sitting in their slots. From here on, prefer f-strings whenever you mix text and values.
